title: Design Rate Limiter
description: Design a distributed rate limiter that protects APIs from abuse and ensures fair usage. Covers rate limiting algorithms (token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window), distributed rate limiting with Redis, counter consistency (local vs global), rate limiting headers, tiered rate limits (per user, per API, per region), and how SAP's RFC gateway throttling and quota management provide a familiar analog.

description.value:
Four primary algorithms exist for rate limiting. Each has different characteristics for memory usage, accuracy, and burst behavior.

1. Token Bucket: A bucket holds tokens (initially N). Tokens are added at a fixed rate R per second. Each request consumes one token. If the bucket is empty, the request is rejected. Burst handling: a client can burst up to N requests (using accumulated tokens). Memory: one counter per key. Pros: simple, handles bursts well (up to bucket size). Cons: requires a background token refill process (or lazy refill on each request). Used by: Amazon API Gateway (burst limit), Stripe API.

2. Leaky Bucket: Requests enter a queue of size N. A background process dequeues requests at a fixed rate R. If the queue is full, the request is rejected. Burst handling: none — requests are processed at a fixed rate regardless of arrival pattern. Pros: smooth output rate — no bursts. Cons: does not allow bursts (even if the client has capacity). Used by: network traffic shaping, message processing pipelines.

3. Fixed Window Counter: Divide time into fixed windows (e.g., 1 minute). Maintain a counter per key per window. If the counter exceeds the limit, reject. Pros: simple, memory-efficient (one counter + window timestamp). Cons: 'boundary issue' — at the window boundary, the counter resets, potentially allowing 2x throughput in a short period (e.g., 100 requests at second 59 + 100 requests at second 61 = 200 requests in 2 seconds with a 100/minute limit).

4. Sliding Window Log: Maintain a time-sorted list of timestamps per key. Count timestamps within the window (e.g., last 60 seconds). If count > limit, reject. Pros: eliminates the boundary issue — precise control. Cons: memory-intensive (stores all timestamps). Optimization: Sliding Window Counter — only store the count for the current window + the previous window's count. Approximate: count = current_window_count + (previous_window_count × overlap_ratio). Used by: Google, most FAANGs.

A single-node rate limiter is trivial (increment a counter in local memory). A distributed rate limiter must coordinate across multiple API server instances. The challenge: if you have 100 API servers and a user's limit is 1000 requests/minute, how do you ensure the total across all 100 servers does not exceed 1000?

Option 1 — Centralized Redis counter: Each API server increments a Redis counter (INCR key) with TTL (EXPIRE key 60). The key is 'user_id:api:rate_limit'. Each server checks the count before accepting the request. Pros: accurate global count, simple to implement. Cons: Redis becomes a bottleneck (every API request hits Redis). Redis latency adds 1-5ms to every request. At 100K req/s, Redis handles 100K INCR operations/second — manageable for a single Redis instance, but the latency adds up.

Option 2 — Local counters with periodic sync: Each API server maintains a local counter with a budget (e.g., each server is allocated 10 req/s of the user's 1000 req/min limit). When the local counter exceeds the budget, the server checks with Redis for a global limit override. Pros: most requests (99%+) are served from the local counter (no Redis hit). Cons: inaccurate at boundaries — a user might exceed the global limit by a small margin (budget allocation is imprecise). This is acceptable for most rate limiting use cases (a user exceeding 1000 req/min by 20 requests is not a problem).

Option 3 — Distributed token bucket with Redis + local cache: Each server maintains a local token bucket for frequently-accessed keys (cached in memory with TTL). The bucket is refilled from a global Redis counter when the local bucket is low. This combines the accuracy of Option 1 with the performance of Option 2. This is the approach used by Google's API Infrastructure and most FAANG-scale rate limiters.

SAP Bridge: SAP's RFC gateway uses the 'gw/sys/cfg/req_limit' profile parameter to limit incoming RFC connections. This is a single-node rate limiter (per application server instance). SAP doesn't have a built-in distributed rate limiter across all application servers. The SAP engineer who proposes a Redis-based global rate limiter for RFC traffic is demonstrating distributed systems thinking beyond SAP's documented capabilities.

Rate limiting should be transparent and informative to the API consumer. The standard approach is to include rate limit information in response headers, so the consumer can adjust their request rate proactively.

Standard rate limit headers (based on IETF draft): (1) RateLimit-Limit — the rate limit value (e.g., 1000 requests per window). (2) RateLimit-Remaining — remaining requests in the current window. (3) RateLimit-Reset — when the rate limit window resets (UNIX timestamp or seconds remaining). (4) Retry-After — how long to wait before retrying (when rate limited). Used in 429 response headers. (5) Custom headers: X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset (GitHub API convention).

Tiered rate limits: Different API consumers have different limits based on their plan/trust level. (1) Unauthenticated users — 10 req/min (IP-based). (2) Free tier (API key) — 100 req/min. (3) Pro tier — 1000 req/min. (4) Enterprise — 10,000 req/min with burst capacity. The rate limiter must check the user's tier before applying the limit. This is typically stored in a Redis hash or the user's database record (cached in Redis).

Hard vs soft limits: Hard limits: request is rejected with 429 status code. Soft limits: request is accepted but the user is warned (header: 'RateLimit-Warning: 10 requests remaining'). Soft limits are preferred for user experience — they give the consumer time to reduce their request rate before being blocked.

details: FAANG Interview Tip: Rate limiter is a common system design question that most candidates answer adequately. The Staff+ signal is: (1) You discuss multiple algorithms and choose based on requirements (not just 'I'll use token bucket'). (2) You discuss distributed consistency (Redis counters, local budgets, sync strategies). (3) You discuss rate limiting headers and user experience ('We should tell the user why they're rate limited and when they can retry'). (4) You discuss tiered rate limits and burst handling. The SAP bridge is straightforward: 'SAP RFC gateway has connection limits per application server. A distributed rate limiter extends this concept across all servers using Redis.'
